Question 4 — Netflix: what works in streaming

Student 25052977

19 June 2026

The brief

A team thinking about launching a streaming service wants a quick read on what sits on Netflix and what rates well: the mix of content by country and type, the genres that dominate, how titles score, how long movies run, and what the catalogue is “about”.

What’s on the platform

Movies vs shows
type titles
MOVIE 3759
SHOW 2047
Top production countries by number of titles
country titles
US 2327
IN 629
GB 406
JP 291
FR 248
CA 216
KR 216
ES 212
DE 139
MX 123

The catalogue is mostly films, and heavily US-led, with India, the UK, Japan and South Korea making up most of the rest. For a new entrant that says the obvious markets are crowded, and that Korean and Japanese content is a meaningful slice worth watching.

Which genres dominate, and do they rate well?

Genre counts and average IMDb score
genre titles avg_imdb
drama 2901 6.66
comedy 2269 6.42
thriller 1178 6.36
action 1053 6.46
romance 958 6.44
documentation 910 7.04
crime 891 6.68
animation 665 6.73
fantasy 631 6.58
family 622 6.34
scifi 587 6.58
european 460 6.54

Drama and comedy are by far the most stocked, but volume and quality don’t line up: the most common genres sit around the middle on IMDb, while smaller genres like documentation and history tend to rate higher. Stocking more of a genre does not make it better content.

How do titles rate?

IMDb rating by type
type n median_imdb mean_imdb
MOVIE 3407 6.4 6.27
SHOW 1876 7.2 7.02

Shows tend to score slightly higher than movies, and the spread is wide for both: plenty of low-rated filler alongside the hits. A new service competing on quality could do well to be more selective than the “stock everything” approach.

How long are the movies?

Movie length is tightly clustered around the standard 90–100 minute feature, tailing off past two hours. There’s little appetite on the platform for very short or very long films.

What is the catalogue about?

The descriptions lean on life, family, love, young and friends: the themes the platform keeps coming back to. Useful for a new service deciding what emotional territory to own.

Takeaways for the team

  • The market is film-heavy and US-dominated, but Korean and Japanese content is a real, growing slice worth targeting.
  • Stocking a genre heavily doesn’t make it rate well: documentaries and history punch above their volume on IMDb.
  • Shows edge out movies on ratings; quality varies widely, so curation beats quantity.
  • Movies cluster at 90–100 minutes; descriptions centre on family, love and youth.
